title: Error Handling Best Practices
description: Follow professional error handling patterns: catch specific exceptions, retry failed operations, chain operations with error propagation, and log errors with structured results.
starterCode:
# Exercise: Error Handling Best Practices

def specific_handler(func, *args):
    """Call func(*args). Return result on success, or (error_type_name, message) tuple on failure."""
    pass

def retry_operation(func, max_retries=3):
    """Call func() up to max_retries times. Return result on success, raise last exception on failure."""
    pass

def chain_operations(operations, data):
    """Process data through list of functions. Stop on first error.
    Return (last_successful_result, error_msg_or_None)."""
    pass

def log_errors(func, *args):
    """Call func(*args). Return dict with success, result, and error keys."""
    pass
hints: Catch specific exceptions (ValueError, TypeError, KeyError) not bare except
Use type(e).__name__ to get the exception class name as a string
In retry_operation, save the last exception and raise it after all retries fail
In chain_operations, keep track of the last successful result before error
Return structured dicts from log_errors so callers can inspect results programmatically
Always include the error message via str(e) for debugging context

testCases.input: chain_operations([lambda x: x * 2, lambda x: x + 1], 5)
testCases.validation: assert chain_operations([lambda x: x * 2, lambda x: x + 1], 5) == (11, None)
